ASSISTANTMESSAGE #62
OK, now let me think about this very carefully. I need to identify the actual root cause of the bug. The issue description says: 1. Characters DO redirect (behavior works) 2. No FALLBACK events in the debug log Let me trace the exact scenario where a redirect happens WITHOUT emitting a fallback event. I've been focused on `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` but let me think about all the places where a redirect can happen: 1. `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` (always emits fallback event) 2. `_handleTalkArrival` (emits fallback event for 'talk_target_moved' reason) 3. `_updateTalkRoute` (emits fallback event for 'talk_target_moved' reason) But looking at the decision engine, there are several cases in `AIEngine._applyDecision` where a character releases their current reservation and starts moving to a new target without ever emitting a fallback event — when switching to 'interact', 'talk', 'idle', or 'move' actions, the old reservation gets dropped but no fallback event fires. Wait, though — the AIEngine does have access to `pushEvent` through `simState`, and it uses this for `talk_rejected` events. So it's technically capable of emitting fallback events in those scenarios, but it just doesn't. The real question is about the specific case where an AI redirects from a full object. Looking at the interact decision logic, there's a check for whether the object is full before proceeding. Let me trace through what happens when a character arrives at an object. The `_handleObjectArrival` method gets called, which then tries to interact with the object... Checking the Engine update loop... Actually, I think I found the issue. The `interact()` method doesn't validate slot occupancy at all—it just performs the state transition if the action exists. So if character B somehow occupies the slot without triggering a state change, then character A arrives and calls interact, the method will succeed regardless of whether the slot is actually available. The real problem is that the slot system and state machine are completely decoupled. When a character uses an object, the state transitions independently of slot management. If both A and B somehow get assigned to the same object (bypassing the reservation system), they'd both successfully call `interact()` and trigger separate state transitions—the second call would execute even though the slot should be occupied. Now I'm refocusing on the actual bug: FALLBACK events aren't showing up in the debug log. I've verified the event type string matches (`'fallback'` in both places) and the debug loop handles it correctly, so the issue must be that `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` isn't actually being called in the first place. Looking at the flow more carefully: the slot reservation system probably prevents most fallback scenarios because characters can't target full objects and skip non-interactable states. But there's a race condition where a character's AI decides to interact with an object, walks toward it, and while walking another character claims it—when the first character arrives and tries to interact with an object now in a non-interactable state like 'tending', the interact call returns null and triggers the fallback event. However, the slot system should catch this: if an object is occupied, `isFull()` returns true and the character never targets it in the first place. The only way this breaks down is if the object transitions to 'tending' state while still having zero occupants—meaning the slot was released but the state changed before the next character could claim it. Or there's a race condition where a character reserves the object while it's idle, but then another character uses it via the debug button (bypassing the slot system entirely), transitioning it to 'tending' before the first character arrives. When the first character finally gets there, the fallback triggers because there's no 'use' transition defined. I'm noticing something in the scene initialization code—it clears pending events... but debug.html has its own separate engine instance, so that shouldn't matter. Let me trace through the event ordering in debug.html's tick loop instead. The simulation updates, then it checks for state changes and logs them, then it processes pending events from the queue. I'm wondering if the fallback event is somehow getting lost or not added to the queue at the right time. Actually, let me step back and look at the git history to find what changed. But first, let me reread the issue more carefully—it mentions checking if `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` is actually being called, verifying the event type string matches between where it's pushed and where it's rendered, and making sure the debug.html tick loop handles fallback events correctly. Let me focus on that third point and check the event type string very carefully in Engine.js. Looking at the consumption loop in debug.html, I can see it's checking for fallback events alongside conversation_start and talk_rejected, and the event type strings all appear to match. So the type mismatch isn't the problem. Now let me examine the consumption loop more closely to see if there's an issue with how the events are being processed. Actually, I think the real issue might be that `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` isn't being called at all. If the slot system is working correctly, characters might never end up in an invalid state in the first place, which would mean the fallback is never triggered. Let me trace through what scenarios would actually cause a character to reach an invalid object state—like if an object auto-transitions while the character is walking toward it, but that would only happen if the idle state had an auto_next property, which it doesn't. The most likely explanation for the visible redirects is that they're coming from the AI engine reassigning characters every 30 seconds via `_applyDecision`, not from the fallback mechanism at all. So a character might reserve an object and start walking, but then 30 seconds later the AI makes a new decision and sends them somewhere else entirely. The fix would be to emit a fallback event whenever the AI engine redirects a character from one object to another. Now I'm looking at the handlers for different action types in `_applyDecision` to see if they all have similar logic for releasing and reassigning targets... Checking the handlers for 'interact', 'move', 'idle', and 'talk'... Actually, I realize the `simState` object passed to the AI engine includes a `pushEvent` function, so the engine can emit events directly. The issue is that when `_applyDecision` releases a previous reservation and assigns a new target, it's not emitting that fallback event that QA is seeing. There are two potential problems here: either `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` rarely gets triggered because the slot system prevents characters from reaching unavailable objects in the first place, or the AIEngine is overriding targets without emitting fallback events. And even if the fallback function does run, there could be a timing issue with how pending events are consumed. But wait—the issue description says Engine.js is pushing fallback events through pendingEvents, which means the authors believe the function IS being called. So the problem must be in how those events are being consumed. Looking at the debug.html consumption loop, it's checking `simEngine.pendingEvents` and shifting events off the array, which looks correct. The real question is whether that `pendingEvents` array in debug.html is actually the same instance that Engine.js is pushing to. I'm realizing the critical issue: in scene.js, the code iterates through `this.engine.pendingEvents` and then replaces the entire array with a new empty one using `this.engine.pendingEvents = []`. This creates a new array reference, so any code holding onto the old array reference would still have the events, but code accessing through `this.engine.pendingEvents` would get the new empty array instead. However, since debug.html runs its own separate engine instance, this shouldn't be the problem. Let me trace through what actually happens in debug.html when a character interacts with the bonsai: the character targets it, reserves the slot, walks over, the interaction succeeds and transitions the bonsai to 'tending', then after the holdMs duration expires, the object's timer fires and auto-transitions it back to 'idle' while the character's interaction timer is still running, and when that timer finally expires the character releases the slot without ever triggering a fallback. Now let me think through a different scenario with the tea_set: it starts idle, a character reserves and walks to it, the interaction transitions it to 'brewing' and marks the character as occupant, the character's interaction timer runs for 15 seconds while the tea_set's own timer also fires after 15 seconds to auto-transition to 'ready', then when the character's timer expires they go idle and release the slot, leaving the tea_set in 'ready' state with no occupants or reservations, so when another character wanders by and sees the 'ready' state with a 'use' transition available, they reserve it and walk over to interact. When the second character arrives and calls interact with 'use', the tea_set transitions back to 'idle', and I'm checking the holdMs calculation—it looks at either the object's interactMs property or defaults to 8000ms, but if the target state has an auto_next timer defined, it uses that instead, so in this case the second character holds the interaction for 6000ms while the tea_set sits in 'idle' state, then releases the slot when done. Now I'm realizing the fallback mechanism might not trigger in normal gameplay—it would only happen if the AI engine requests an interaction with an unavailable object. But actually, AI decisions can fire while a character is walking, not just when idle, so if a character is walking to an object and another AI timer fires, they could request a different interaction before arriving at the first one. The real issue is that when the AI redirects a character mid-walk to a new target, no fallback event gets emitted—it's just a silent redirect, not actually a "fallback" scenario in the sense of an object becoming unavailable. Re-reading the issue more carefully, it mentions redirects happening "when targets are full," which points to the slot system preventing characters from reserving full objects in the first place. But if that's working, how could a character end up needing to redirect? There might be a race condition I'm missing, or the re-evaluation logic from FRG-224 is handling mid-walk failures differently than I'm seeing in the code. Let me check if the issue is actually in how debug.html filters and logs events. Looking at the event processing loop, it's checking for specific event types like conversation_start, talk_rejected, and fallback before adding them to the log. The fallback event might be getting generated but filtered out somewhere in this pipeline. Wait—I think I found it. The event consumption happens inside the `!paused && lastTs !== null` block, which means on the very first frame when `lastTs` is null, `simEngine.update()` never runs, so no events get generated or consumed at all. On the second frame, `lastTs` is set and everything kicks in. That's actually correct behavior though. Let me think about this differently—maybe the events are being emitted but the log isn't rendering properly due to a display issue. Actually, I should step back and look at what the issue is really saying. The investigation hints suggest that `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` might not be getting called at all. Let me trace through the AI engine logic: when it redirects a character because the first object is unavailable, it calls `_applyDecision` which doesn't emit a fallback event. The real scenario to check is when targets are full and the character tries to redirect. I'm realizing the core issue: the AIEngine checks `!obj.isFull()` before reserving, so if an object is full at decision time, the character just fails to assign—no redirect happens. But there's a race condition: what if the AI sends a character to object X when slots are available, then another character grabs the last slot while the first is walking? When the first character arrives and calls `interact()`, the slot system shows X as full, but `interact()` doesn't actually check slots—it just does the state transition. So both characters could end up calling `interact()` on the same object if they're not properly checking availability. Looking at `_handleObjectArrival`, when a character arrives, it calls `interact()` first, and only if that succeeds does it convert the reservation to active occupancy via `arrive()`. If `interact()` fails, it releases the reservation and tries a fallback object. So the question is what makes `interact()` fail in the first place. For `interact()` to return null, the object would need to be in a state without a 'use' transition. But since characters only reserve objects that are in 'idle' (which has 'use'), and 'idle' states don't have auto_next transitions, the object can't transition away while the character is walking. This means `interact()` should never fail for a properly reserved object—the fallback logic would only trigger if someone manipulates the object via debug buttons during the walk. So whatever "redirect" behavior the QA team is seeing must be coming from the AI engine itself, not from this reservation system. Now I'm looking at the `_doWander` function to understand how the 30% object interaction case works and whether that might be another source of redirects. I'm noticing a potential race condition: `reserve()` gets called after `moveTo()` succeeds, but what if another character claims the slot in between? In single-threaded JavaScript this shouldn't happen within a synchronous function, and Promise callbacks don't interrupt mid-function either. But I need to think about what happens when multiple characters are processed in the same update loop... Actually, looking at how the Engine iterates through characters sequentially, Character A would complete its entire `_doWander` call (including both `moveTo()` and `reserve()`) before Character B even starts. So there's no race condition here. This means `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` rarely triggers during normal simulation—the "redirect" behavior QA is seeing is actually the AI engine reassigning character targets. The real fix should be emitting fallback events from the AIEngine whenever it overrides a character's current object target. Looking at the AIEngine implementation... I'm looking at the fallback event structure and how it renders—it displays something like "Maple: bonsai unavailable → bookshelf" to show when a character gets redirected away from an object. This makes sense for cases where the AI decides the object is no longer available or the character needs to do something else instead. But I'm realizing the real issue is more nuanced—when a target object is full, the character never gets assigned to it in the first place, so there's no fallback needed. The tricky case is when a character is *already* heading toward one object and the AI reassigns them to a different one mid-stream. That's when I need to handle releasing the previous reservation before assigning the new target. Looking at the interact case, if the character has a different targetObjectId than the new object they're being sent to, I release that old reservation first, then check if the new target is actually available before committing to it. But wait—I'm realizing there's another scenario here. What if the AI decides to interact with the same object the character is already walking toward? In that case, the targetObjectId check wouldn't trigger the release logic, and if that object is already full from the character's own reservation, the availability check would fail silently with no fallback event fired. Let me step back and reconsider what's actually happening. The issue mentions the code for pushing fallback events exists and the rendering is set up, yet QA observed zero events across 14+ hours. That suggests either the function triggering these events isn't being called at all, or there's a gap in the logic where redirects happen without emitting the event. Maybe the real fix is ensuring the AIEngine emits a fallback event whenever it overrides a character's current target. Let me look at yet another angle. What if the events are being emitted but `logDirty` isn't being set properly, so the log doesn't re-render? I'm checking the consumption logic - when an event comes in, it gets added to the front of the log array, the length is capped, and `logDirty` is set to true. Then the render function should pick up on that flag and update the display. Actually, I'm realizing the render function is called every frame outside the pause check, so it should always be checking that dirty flag. Even if the sim is paused, the event log still renders when dirty. This flow looks correct to me. Let me step back and think about this differently - maybe the issue is simpler than I'm making it. The problem statement says FALLBACK events never appear in the debug event log, so let me focus on what's actually happening with those specific events. The most straightforward explanation is that `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` simply isn't being called at all. If the slot reservation system is working properly, characters should only ever arrive at objects they've reserved, and those objects should be in the correct state when the character gets there. That means `interact()` should always succeed, so the fallback path never triggers. The real issue is that when the AIEngine reassigns a character mid-walk to a different target, it's not emitting any fallback event. I need to add a `'fallback'` event emission in `AIEngine._applyDecision` when it overrides a character's current target. But wait—let me reconsider the scenario from the issue. If Nyx targets the bonsai while Maple is using it, and the AI's `!obj.isFull()` check prevents the assignment in the first place, then Nyx wouldn't get reassigned at all. So either the redirect is happening through a different mechanism, or I'm misunderstanding what's actually occurring. Actually, I think the key is that the AI decision gets logged even if the walk doesn't execute. The `onDecision` callback fires and records that Nyx was told to interact with the bonsai, but since it's full, the walk never starts. The async fetch completes 12. Promise callback fires: _applyDecision() is called, might push events via simState.pushEvent (for talk_rejected) 13. But these events are pushed AFTER the consumption loop ran! 14. They'll be consumed in the next frame's consumption loop So talk_rejected events from the AIEngine would show up one frame later, which is fine. Looking at the commits, FRG-224 added the fallback logic with `_fallbackToAlternativeObject`, and FRG-222 added the interaction slot system. The slot system prevents characters from targeting occupied objects, which means the fallback condition that FRG-224 was designed to handle almost never gets triggered anymore. So the real issue is that we need a different mechanism to emit fallback events when the AIEngine overrides a character's target. Reconsidering the QA observation about characters redirecting—it's likely that Nyx's AI requests an occupied object, stays idle since it can't reserve a slot, then the wander timer fires and picks a different object. To an observer, this looks like a fallback, but it's actually just the wander system kicking in after the AI request fails. The other scenario is that Nyx already has a reservation and is walking toward the object, but then the AI's decision timer fires with a new instruction, causing the AIEngine to release the old reservation and send Nyx somewhere else. This is a true redirect from the AI engine, and we need to emit fallback events in the `_applyDecision` method when it overrides an existing target—specifically in the 'interact', 'move', 'idle', and 'talk' cases—to capture when a character redirects away from a full or newly-unavailable target. needs its own fallback event. With capacity=1 and char having reservation, `isFull()` = 1 >= 1 = true! - So `!obj.isFull()` is FALSE → new assignment fails - But char already had the reservation! So char continues heading to Y - Hmm actually there's a bug here: the "if new assignment fails" path clears nothing, so char continues with existing When the character's target matches the object they're trying to interact with, the first release block skips, and the second reserve block also skips because the object is already full from their own reservation—so they just keep walking toward it, which is the correct behavior. The arrival check fires when the character is idle and has a targetObjectId set. But when does the character actually become idle after finishing their walk? Looking at the Character update logic, the character transitions to idle once the path is empty, and that's when the wander timer resets. So the arrival handler should trigger at that point—but maybe the event emission is getting lost somewhere in that sequence. The character becomes idle when `this.path.length === 0`. This happens inside `char.update(deltaMs)`. But `_handleObjectArrival` is checked BEFORE `char.update()`: ```javascript for (const char of this.characters) { ... if (char.action === 'idle' && char.targetObjectId) { this._handleObjectArrival(char); // checked BEFORE update } ... char.update(deltaMs); // this sets action to 'idle' when path ends } ``` So when the character's path finishes in frame N's `char.update()`, the character becomes idle. Then in frame N+1, the arrival check fires. This is the expected behavior. But what if the character is already at the destination tile when `moveTo()` is called? If the path is empty because they're already adjacent, `moveTo()` returns false and the character tries a different tile instead. Now I'm wondering what happens if `_findAdjacentWalkable()` can't find any adjacent tiles to walk to. I'm realizing there's a potential bug here: if the character is already standing on an adjacent tile to the target object, `findPath` returns an empty array since start equals goal, which makes `moveTo` return false. This prevents the reservation and targetObjectId from being set, so the character never actually interacts with the object—it just gets stuck without triggering the arrival handler. But stepping back, I think the real issue is that `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` rarely gets called during normal gameplay because the slot reservation system prevents double-booking in the first place. The redirects QA is seeing are likely coming from the AIEngine reassigning characters mid-walk, not from this fallback logic. The fix should be straightforward: emit fallback events in the AIEngine when it overrides a character's existing target. That said, `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` can still trigger through debug buttons that bypass the slot system, so I need to consider edge cases where manual state changes happen while a character is walking to an object. Looking at a concrete example: if a user manually puts an object into a state without a 'use' transition while a character is en route, the character arrives and `interact()` fails, which does trigger the fallback. But QA ran 14+ hours of simulation without seeing fallback events, which suggests they weren't hitting these manual debug scenarios—they were observing pure AI-driven redirects that currently don't emit anything. With single-capacity objects, the object can't change state while a character walks toward it. With multi-capacity objects like kotatsu, multiple characters can reserve it, but the first one to arrive should still find it in a usable state. So the real issue is that when AIEngine decides to override a character's target, it's not emitting a fallback event—that's the gap I need to fix. The only way it could disappear is if something else drains the queue between the update call and the event processing—but that shouldn't be possible since JavaScript is single-threaded and the animation frame callback runs atomically. Even microtasks can't interrupt a synchronous task; they'd only fire after the entire callback returns. So the async AI callback definitely isn't stealing events mid-tick. I think the real issue is that fallback events simply aren't being generated in normal operation. The slot reservation system prevents characters from ever reaching unavailable objects in the first place, so `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` rarely gets called. The redirects QA is seeing are probably AI engine reassignments that don't emit fallback events at all. For `'move'`, `'idle'`, and `'talk'`, I only emit fallback events when there's an existing `targetObjectId` to transition from, otherwise there's nothing meaningful to log. The tricky case is when an `'interact'` action tries to redirect to a new object but fails because it's full—the old reservation gets released, the new assignment fails, and the character ends up with no target. This should still emit a fallback event showing the transition from the old object to idle, since the AI attempted a redirect that didn't work out. I'm working through the fallback event logic for each decision type—when a character abandons an object target, I need to emit the right transition showing where they came from and where they're going. For interact decisions, I'll capture the new target (or null if reservation fails); for move, idle, and talk, I'll emit transitions from the old object to their new state. I'm also considering whether to reuse existing reason codes or create new ones, since scenarios like the AI reassigning to a different object don't quite fit 'object_unavailable'. But there's a race condition: what if the AI sends a character to object X when slots are available, then another character grabs the last slot while the first is walking? When the first character arrives and calls `interact()`, the slot system shows X as full, but `interact()` doesn't actually check slots\u2014it just does the state transition. So both characters could end up calling `interact()` on the same object if they're not properly checking availability.\n\nLooking at `_handleObjectArrival`, when a character arrives, it calls `interact()` first, and only if that succeeds does it convert the reservation to active occupancy via `arrive()`. If `interact()` fails, it releases the reservation and tries a fallback object. So the question is what makes `interact()` fail in the first place.\n\nFor `interact()` to return null, the object would need to be in a state without a 'use' transition. But since characters only reserve objects that are in 'idle' (which has 'use'), and 'idle' states don't have auto_next transitions, the object can't transition away while the character is walking. This means `interact()` should never fail for a properly reserved object\u2014the fallback logic would only trigger if someone manipulates the object via debug buttons during the walk. So whatever \"redirect\" behavior the QA team is seeing must be coming from the AI engine itself, not from this reservation system.\n\nNow I'm looking at the `_doWander` function to understand how the 30% object interaction case works and whether that might be another source of redirects.\n\nI'm noticing a potential race condition: `reserve()` gets called after `moveTo()` succeeds, but what if another character claims the slot in between? In single-threaded JavaScript this shouldn't happen within a synchronous function, and Promise callbacks don't interrupt mid-function either. But I need to think about what happens when multiple characters are processed in the same update loop...\n\nActually, looking at how the Engine iterates through characters sequentially, Character A would complete its entire `_doWander` call (including both `moveTo()` and `reserve()`) before Character B even starts. So there's no race condition here.\n\nThis means `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` rarely triggers during normal simulation\u2014the \"redirect\" behavior QA is seeing is actually the AI engine reassigning character targets. The real fix should be emitting fallback events from the AIEngine whenever it overrides a character's current object target.\n\nLooking at the AIEngine implementation... I'm looking at the fallback event structure and how it renders\u2014it displays something like \"Maple: bonsai unavailable \u2192 bookshelf\" to show when a character gets redirected away from an object. This makes sense for cases where the AI decides the object is no longer available or the character needs to do something else instead.\n\nBut I'm realizing the real issue is more nuanced\u2014when a target object is full, the character never gets assigned to it in the first place, so there's no fallback needed. The tricky case is when a character is *already* heading toward one object and the AI reassigns them to a different one mid-stream. That's when I need to handle releasing the previous reservation before assigning the new target.\n\nLooking at the interact case, if the character has a different targetObjectId than the new object they're being sent to, I release that old reservation first, then check if the new target is actually available before committing to it.\n\nBut wait\u2014I'm realizing there's another scenario here. What if the AI decides to interact with the same object the character is already walking toward? In that case, the targetObjectId check wouldn't trigger the release logic, and if that object is already full from the character's own reservation, the availability check would fail silently with no fallback event fired.\n\nLet me step back and reconsider what's actually happening. The issue mentions the code for pushing fallback events exists and the rendering is set up, yet QA observed zero events across 14+ hours. That suggests either the function triggering these events isn't being called at all, or there's a gap in the logic where redirects happen without emitting the event. Maybe the real fix is ensuring the AIEngine emits a fallback event whenever it overrides a character's current target. Let me look at yet another angle. What if the events are being emitted but `logDirty` isn't being set properly, so the log doesn't re-render? I'm checking the consumption logic - when an event comes in, it gets added to the front of the log array, the length is capped, and `logDirty` is set to true. Then the render function should pick up on that flag and update the display.\n\nActually, I'm realizing the render function is called every frame outside the pause check, so it should always be checking that dirty flag. Even if the sim is paused, the event log still renders when dirty. This flow looks correct to me.\n\nLet me step back and think about this differently - maybe the issue is simpler than I'm making it. The problem statement says FALLBACK events never appear in the debug event log, so let me focus on what's actually happening with those specific events.\n\nThe most straightforward explanation is that `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` simply isn't being called at all. If the slot reservation system is working properly, characters should only ever arrive at objects they've reserved, and those objects should be in the correct state when the character gets there. That means `interact()` should always succeed, so the fallback path never triggers.\n\nThe real issue is that when the AIEngine reassigns a character mid-walk to a different target, it's not emitting any fallback event. I need to add a `'fallback'` event emission in `AIEngine._applyDecision` when it overrides a character's current target.\n\nBut wait\u2014let me reconsider the scenario from the issue. If Nyx targets the bonsai while Maple is using it, and the AI's `!obj.isFull()` check prevents the assignment in the first place, then Nyx wouldn't get reassigned at all. So either the redirect is happening through a different mechanism, or I'm misunderstanding what's actually occurring.\n\nActually, I think the key is that the AI decision gets logged even if the walk doesn't execute. The `onDecision` callback fires and records that Nyx was told to interact with the bonsai, but since it's full, the walk never starts. So the \"targeting\" is just in the decision log, not in actual character state.\n\nA fallback event could help clarify this\u2014when an interaction fails due to capacity, I could log that Nyx attempted the action but the object was unavailable, then fell back to idle or wandering behavior.\n\nThe fallback event structure already has fields for character, reason, source object, and destination, but I'd need to add a new reason type for \"object full\" alongside the existing `'object_unavailable'` and `'talk_target_moved'` reasons.\n\nLooking at the investigation checklist, the key issue is verifying that the event type string matches consistently between where fallback events are pushed and where they're rendered in the debug view.\n\nChecking the event type string... Checking the event structure across different code paths - the `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` method also pushes events with `type: 'fallback'` and `reason: 'object_unavailable'`, using either the alternative object's name or null for the `toLabel` field depending on whether a fallback target exists.\n\nSo the type strings are consistent. Let me reconsider what could cause fallback events to never appear over 14+ hours - either the function isn't being called at all, the events are being pushed but somehow consumed before debug.html can read them, or there's a timing issue where the events aren't available when the debug interface tries to access them.\n\nThe most suspicious angle is async behavior from the AI engine. Since `_applyDecision` runs as a Promise callback, it could potentially push an event after the consumption loop in the tick cycle has already run, meaning the event would sit in the queue until the next frame - but that should still be visible eventually.\n\nLet me trace through the actual execution order... simEngine.update(realDelta) \u2192 aiEngine.update() \u2192 decrements timers, maybe fires _requestDecision() which creates a Promise (async fetch)\n3. simEngine.update() returns\n4. Auto-transitions logged\n5. while (simEngine.pendingEvents.length > 0) consumes all current events\n6. simEngine.characters.forEach(...) updates character cards\n7. lastTs = ts\n8. renderEventLog()\n9. tick() returns\n10. ... some time passes ...\n11. The async fetch completes\n12. Promise callback fires: _applyDecision() is called, might push events via simState.pushEvent (for talk_rejected)\n13. But these events are pushed AFTER the consumption loop ran!\n14. They'll be consumed in the next frame's consumption loop\n\nSo talk_rejected events from the AIEngine would show up one frame later, which is fine. Same goes for any fallback events the AIEngine might trigger. This timing doesn't lose events.\n\nLet me check the git history to see what actually changed and broke the fallback events. I can't access git directly here, but I can look at the recent commits to trace what might have gone wrong.\n\nLooking at the commits, FRG-224 added the fallback logic with `_fallbackToAlternativeObject`, and FRG-222 added the interaction slot system. The slot system prevents characters from targeting occupied objects, which means the fallback condition that FRG-224 was designed to handle almost never gets triggered anymore.\n\nSo the real issue is that we need a different mechanism to emit fallback events when the AIEngine overrides a character's target. Reconsidering the QA observation about characters redirecting\u2014it's likely that Nyx's AI requests an occupied object, stays idle since it can't reserve a slot, then the wander timer fires and picks a different object. To an observer, this looks like a fallback, but it's actually just the wander system kicking in after the AI request fails.\n\nThe other scenario is that Nyx already has a reservation and is walking toward the object, but then the AI's decision timer fires with a new instruction, causing the AIEngine to release the old reservation and send Nyx somewhere else. This is a true redirect from the AI engine, and we need to emit fallback events in the `_applyDecision` method when it overrides an existing target\u2014specifically in the 'interact', 'move', 'idle', and 'talk' cases\u2014to capture when a character redirects away from a full or newly-unavailable target. needs its own fallback event. This would be when the AI says \"interact X\" but X is full, so the character doesn't get assigned. That's not really a \"redirect\" though...\n\nActually, let me look at this from the acceptance criteria perspective:\n- \"FALLBACK events visible in debug event log when character redirects\"\n- \"Events show reason and from/to objects\"\n\nThe acceptance criteria is about WHEN CHARACTER REDIRECTS. A redirect happens when:\n1. Character was going to object A, now going to object B (or idle)\n2. This can happen via `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` (already emits event)\n3. OR via AI engine overriding the target (currently NO event)\n\nSo the fix should:\n1. Ensure `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` IS being called (or find why it's not being called in practice)\n2. OR emit fallback events from the AIEngine when it redirects characters\n\nGiven the investigation hints, I think the issue creators believe `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` IS being called and the events ARE being pushed, but they're not appearing in the log. But from my analysis, the function might rarely be called due to the slot system.\n\nLet me look at this from yet another angle. What if `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` IS being called (e.g., via the AI manually overriding the debug buttons to put the object in an incompatible state), but the events are being lost? One scenario where events could be lost: events are pushed to `pendingEvents` during `simEngine.update()` BUT `simEngine.pendingEvents` is being cleared or replaced somewhere. Actually, I need to check what `aiEngine.update()` does exactly and whether it's clearing the pending events queue.\n\nDuring the update cycle, `aiEngine.update()` just handles timer decrements and initiates async fetch requests\u2014the actual callbacks fire later. Meanwhile, `objectEngine.tick()` might push emoji events, and for each character, `_handleObjectArrival`, `_handleTalkArrival`, and `_updateTalkRoute` could all push fallback events. The question is whether these events are persisting through the entire update cycle or getting lost somewhere.\n\nI'm checking the actual behavior against what should happen: fallback events should be created by `_fallbackToAlternativeObject`, marked with the right type, and consumed by the debug loop. But QA reports they never show up, which means either the fallback function isn't being called at all because the slot system is working too well, or there's a subtle bug in how the events are being consumed. Let me trace through the consumption logic...\n\nExamining the event loop...\n\nThe loop pulls events from `pendingEvents` and logs conversation starts, rejections, and fallbacks. But wait\u2014what if events ARE queued but the loop isn't actually draining them? Could `pendingEvents` be growing faster than it's being consumed, or is something preventing the shift operation from working as expected?\n\nActually, looking at the loop body more carefully, it only shifts and logs\u2014nothing pushes new events back into `pendingEvents`, so the drain should work correctly. But I'm wondering if there's a timing issue where `pendingEvents` gets cleared between when events are pushed during `simEngine.update()` and when the while loop tries to consume them in the same frame.\n\nTracing through the tick function, there's nothing between the update call and the consumption loop that would clear the queue, so any events pushed during update should definitely be consumed.\n\nI'm now realizing the actual issue: **`_fallbackToAlternativeObject` probably never gets called during normal simulation because the slot reservation system prevents characters from arriving at unavailable objects in the first place**. The path where `interact()` returns null and triggers the fallback is essentially unreachable in practice.\n\nWhat the QA team is seeing as \"redirects\" are actually the AI engine reassigning character targets, not the object arrival fallback mechanism kicking in.\n\nTo fix this and get FALLBACK events showing up in the debug log, I need to emit those events from the AIEngine itself when it overrides a character's target, specifically in the `_applyDecision` method when handling the 'interact' case and the AI redirects the character.\n\nBut I'm realizing the semantics here are off\u2014when the AI reassigns a character, it's not because the object is unavailable, it's because the AI changed its mind about what the character should do. So maybe I should use a different event type or message that better reflects what's actually happening.\n\nLet me reconsider the acceptance criteria: I need FALLBACK events visible in the debug log whenever a character redirects, showing the reason and which objects are involved (from and to). The specific scenario mentioned is when targets are full, but with the current reservation system, that shouldn't happen since reservations prevent overbooking. So I'm trying to figure out what scenarios actually trigger redirects and what the right terminology is for describing them.\n\nActually, I think the core requirement is just getting those FALLBACK events to fire whenever any redirect occurs. The simplest solution would be to emit these events directly in the AIEngine whenever a character's target gets overridden or released, rather than trying to handle it elsewhere. I need to add event emissions in the decision-applying logic wherever a character's current target is being changed.\n\nWriting the fallback event logic...\n\nI'm realizing the reason string needs to match what the rendering code expects, otherwise it'll just display the raw string. I could reuse `'object_unavailable'` for AI redirects since the object becoming unavailable is technically why the redirect happened, or I could add a new reason type but then I'd need to update the rendering logic to handle it properly.\n\nNow I'm mapping out what actually happens when the AI tries to interact with a full object. If the character switches targets, the old reservation gets released but the new assignment fails because the object is full, leaving the character with no target and falling back to idle. I need to trace through the different scenarios\u2014when the AI picks a different target versus the same target, and what happens in each case when that target is full. AI doesn't reassign (Y was already targeted)\n- Wait, this case: char is heading to Y (reserved), AI says \"interact Y\" again...\n  - `character.targetObjectId === obj.id` \u2192 skip the \"different object\" check\n  - `!obj.isFull()` \u2192 Y is NOT full (char has reservation) \u2192 wait, but obj.reserve might fail\n  - Actually wait: `obj.isFull()` includes the char's own reservation. With capacity=1 and char having reservation, `isFull()` = 1 >= 1 = true!\n  - So `!obj.isFull()` is FALSE \u2192 new assignment fails\n  - But char already had the reservation! So char continues heading to Y\n  - Hmm actually there's a bug here: the \"if new assignment fails\" path clears nothing, so char continues with existing\n\nWhen the character's target matches the object they're trying to interact with, the first release block skips, and the second reserve block also skips because the object is already full from their own reservation\u2014so they just keep walking toward it, which is the correct behavior.\n\nFor scenario C, when the AI commands something else like moving or talking to a different target, the previous reservation gets released and the character falls back to Y.\n\nThe real question is where fallback events should fire. Looking at what QA actually observes, the most frequent visible redirects happen when the AI makes a new decision every 30 seconds and changes the character's target. I need to add fallback event emission in the AIEngine where the character's object target gets overridden.\n\nActually, let me reconsider\u2014the issue says fallback behavior works but events don't show up. Maybe `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` is being called but the events aren't being emitted properly. Let me check the Engine.js code around arrival handling...\n\nOh, I see it now. The arrival check fires when the character is idle and has a targetObjectId set. But when does the character actually become idle after finishing their walk? Looking at the Character update logic, the character transitions to idle once the path is empty, and that's when the wander timer resets. So the arrival handler should trigger at that point\u2014but maybe the event emission is getting lost somewhere in that sequence. The character becomes idle when `this.path.length === 0`. This happens inside `char.update(deltaMs)`.\n\nBut `_handleObjectArrival` is checked BEFORE `char.update()`:\n\n```javascript\nfor (const char of this.characters) {\n    ...\n    if (char.action === 'idle' && char.targetObjectId) {\n        this._handleObjectArrival(char);  // checked BEFORE update\n    }\n    ...\n    char.update(deltaMs);  // this sets action to 'idle' when path ends\n}\n```\n\nSo when the character's path finishes in frame N's `char.update()`, the character becomes idle. Then in frame N+1, the arrival check fires. This is the expected behavior.\n\nBut what if the character is already at the destination tile when `moveTo()` is called? If the path is empty because they're already adjacent, `moveTo()` returns false and the character tries a different tile instead. Now I'm wondering what happens if `_findAdjacentWalkable()` can't find any adjacent tiles to walk to. I'm realizing there's a potential bug here: if the character is already standing on an adjacent tile to the target object, `findPath` returns an empty array since start equals goal, which makes `moveTo` return false. This prevents the reservation and targetObjectId from being set, so the character never actually interacts with the object\u2014it just gets stuck without triggering the arrival handler.\n\nBut stepping back, I think the real issue is that `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` rarely gets called during normal gameplay because the slot reservation system prevents double-booking in the first place. The redirects QA is seeing are likely coming from the AIEngine reassigning characters mid-walk, not from this fallback logic.\n\nThe fix should be straightforward: emit fallback events in the AIEngine when it overrides a character's existing target. That said, `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` can still trigger through debug buttons that bypass the slot system, so I need to consider edge cases where manual state changes happen while a character is walking to an object.\n\nLooking at a concrete example: if a user manually puts an object into a state without a 'use' transition while a character is en route, the character arrives and `interact()` fails, which does trigger the fallback. But QA ran 14+ hours of simulation without seeing fallback events, which suggests they weren't hitting these manual debug scenarios\u2014they were observing pure AI-driven redirects that currently don't emit anything.\n\nLet me trace through whether an AI character can end up with a stale `targetObjectId` pointing to an object in an unusable state. With single-capacity objects, the object can't change state while a character walks toward it. With multi-capacity objects like kotatsu, multiple characters can reserve it, but the first one to arrive should still find it in a usable state. So the real issue is that when AIEngine decides to override a character's target, it's not emitting a fallback event\u2014that's the gap I need to fix. The kotatsu's state machine has a design flaw where the second character using it in 'cozy' state transitions it back to 'idle', which doesn't make sense for a two-person object. But that's a separate issue from the fallback bug I'm investigating. The real problem is that `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` almost never gets triggered during normal simulation because the slot reservation system prevents characters from even attempting to interact with full objects in the first place.\n\nWhat I'm actually seeing as \"redirects\" in QA are cases where the AIEngine reassigns a character to a different target mid-walk\u2014the AI changes its mind about which object to use\u2014and those reassignments don't generate fallback events. The fix should be to emit fallback events when the AI overrides an existing target assignment.\n\nBut wait, let me verify whether `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` is even being called at all, or if the events are somehow getting lost in the timing.\n\nExamining the animation loop...\n\nChecking the event queue timing...\n\nIf a fallback event is pushed during `simEngine.update()`, it should be sitting in `pendingEvents` right after, ready for the while loop to consume it. The only way it could disappear is if something else drains the queue between the update call and the event processing\u2014but that shouldn't be possible since JavaScript is single-threaded and the animation frame callback runs atomically.\n\nEven microtasks can't interrupt a synchronous task; they'd only fire after the entire callback returns. So the async AI callback definitely isn't stealing events mid-tick.\n\nI think the real issue is that fallback events simply aren't being generated in normal operation. The slot reservation system prevents characters from ever reaching unavailable objects in the first place, so `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` rarely gets called. The redirects QA is seeing are probably AI engine reassignments that don't emit fallback events at all.\n\nThe fix is to emit fallback events directly from the AI decision logic when a character gets reassigned to a different target.\n\nWriting the implementation...\n\nActually, I'm second-guessing the reason code\u2014'object_unavailable' doesn't quite capture when the AI just changes its mind mid-action. I should probably introduce a new 'ai_redirect' reason and update the debug renderer to handle it, so the fallback event displays something more accurate than the current mapping. I think `'ai_redirect'` works well as a reason code, which I can render as \"redirected\" in the debug view. The key scenario to handle is when the AI sends a character to a completely different object\u2014like redirecting Nyx from the bonsai to the bookshelf\u2014so the event clearly shows the transition with a meaningful reason for debugging purposes.\n\nNow I'm working through which action types should emit fallback events: `'interact'` when targeting a different object definitely needs one, but if the target is the same object or the assignment fails because it's full, I should show the redirect attempt and its outcome. For `'move'`, `'idle'`, and `'talk'`, I only emit fallback events when there's an existing `targetObjectId` to transition from, otherwise there's nothing meaningful to log.\n\nThe tricky case is when an `'interact'` action tries to redirect to a new object but fails because it's full\u2014the old reservation gets released, the new assignment fails, and the character ends up with no target. This should still emit a fallback event showing the transition from the old object to idle, since the AI attempted a redirect that didn't work out. I'm working through the fallback event logic for each decision type\u2014when a character abandons an object target, I need to emit the right transition showing where they came from and where they're going. For interact decisions, I'll capture the new target (or null if reservation fails); for move, idle, and talk, I'll emit transitions from the old object to their new state. I'm also considering whether to reuse existing reason codes or create new ones, since scenarios like the AI reassigning to a different object don't quite fit 'object_unavailable'.\n\nThe practical approach is to use a new reason like 'plan_changed' and add a mapping for it in debug.html, since the catch-all already handles unknown reasons. For the minimal fix that meets the criteria, I just need to emit fallback events when the character's target changes and ensure debug.html renders the reason and from/to objects correctly. The reason here IS that bonsai is unavailable (Maple is using it). So 'object_unavailable' is actually appropriate for the AI case too!\n\nBut there are other cases: AI decides to talk instead (reason: not object_unavailable), AI decides to move to a random tile (reason: not object_unavailable), AI decides to idle (reason: not object_unavailable).\n\nFor these cases, maybe 'plan_changed' makes more sense. But then I need to update debug.html rendering.\n\nOR... I could just use 'object_unavailable' for all of them. The display would say \"unavailable\" which is close enough (from the AI's perspective, the character is being redirected because of something going on with the object).\n\nActually no. Let me just be accurate. I'll use 'object_unavailable' when AI redirects to another object because it became unavailable or the plan changed, and 'plan_changed' when redirecting to idle/move/talk. I need to add a rendering entry in debug.html for 'plan_changed' too.\n\nActually, thinking about the minimal change needed: the acceptance criteria asks for FALLBACK events when a character REDIRECTS. A redirect happens when the character changes their destination, and the most visible case is when the AI reassigns the target. So I just need to emit a fallback event in AIEngine._applyDecision when it releases the previous object reservation and assigns a new one.\n\nWriting the actual fix now...\n\nImplementing the interact case handler... I'm finding an adjacent walkable tile near the object, then moving the character there and attempting to reserve a slot. If the reservation succeeds, I set the character's target and pending action, and emit a fallback event if they were redirected from another object.\n\nNow I'm handling the case where the new target is also unavailable\u2014the character goes idle and I emit a fallback event with no destination. But I'm realizing this only covers redirects between objects, not initial assignments or other action types like idle or move commands.\n\nAdding the idle case...\n\nNow for the move case, I need to handle the same cleanup when a character switches to moving freely\u2014releasing any object reservation and emitting the fallback event if they were previously targeting something. I need to update the debug handler to recognize the new 'plan_changed' reason, and then carefully review where to insert these fallback events in each case to make sure I'm capturing the right context\u2014particularly whether the talk target should be included in the event or left null. Looking at the actual fallback events being pushed in Engine.js across multiple lines, they all consistently use `type: 'fallback'`, but in debug.html the event type check appears to be looking for something different\u2014that's likely where the mismatch is happening. USERMESSAGE #65
✓ TOOL RESULT
SUCCESS 
   160→  _applyDecision(character, decision, characters, objects, simState) {
   161→    if (!decision?.action) return;
   162→
   163→    switch (decision.action) {
   164→      case 'move':
   165→        if (decision.targetTile) {
   166→          // Release any pending object reservation before moving freely
   167→          if (character.targetObjectId) {
   168→            const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);
   169→            if (prevObj) prevObj.release(character.name);
   170→            character.targetObjectId = null;
   171→          }
   172→          character.moveTo(decision.targetTile.tx, decision.targetTile.ty);
   173→        }
   174→        break;
   175→
   176→      case 'interact': {
   177→        const obj = objects.find(o => o.id === decision.targetId);
   178→        if (obj) {
   179→          // If already heading to a different object, release that reservation first
   180→          if (character.targetObjectId && character.targetObjectId !== obj.id) {
   181→            const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);
   182→            if (prevObj) prevObj.release(character.name);
   183→            character.targetObjectId = null;
   184→          }
   185→
   186→          // Only commit if a slot is available and object is currently interactable
   187→          const stateDef = obj.states[obj.state];
   188→          if (!obj.isFull() && stateDef?.transitions?.use) {
   189→            // Walk to an adjacent walkable tile (same pattern as wander engine)
   190→            const adjTile = simState?.findAdjacentWalkable?.(obj.tx, obj.ty);
   191→            if (adjTile && character.moveTo(adjTile.tx, adjTile.ty)) {
   192→              if (obj.reserve(character.name)) { // claim slot; guard against any race
   193→                character.targetObjectId = obj.id;
   194→                character.pendingAction  = 'use';
   195→              }
   196→            }
   197→          }
   198→        }
   199→        break;
   200→      }
   201→
   202→      case 'idle':
   203→        // Release any pending reservation, then stay put for one full decision interval
   204→        if (character.targetObjectId) {
   205→          const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);
   206→          if (prevObj) prevObj.release(character.name);
   207→          character.targetObjectId = null;
   208→        }
   209→        character.wanderTimer = this.decisionIntervalMs;
   210→        break;
   211→
   212→      case 'talk': {
   213→        const target = characters.find(c => c.label === decision.targetCharacter);
   214→        if (target) {
   215→          // Evaluate Phase: check if B can accept a conversation request.
   216→          // Accept if idle or walking; reject if interacting, talking, or otherwise busy.
   217→          const canAccept = target.action === 'idle' || target.action === 'walking';
   218→
   219→          if (!canAccept) {
   220→            // B is busy — log the rejection and let A fall back to idle/wander
   221→            if (simState?.pushEvent) {
   222→              simState.pushEvent({
   223→                type:           'talk_rejected',
   224→                initiatorLabel: character.label,
   225→                targetLabel:    target.label,
   226→                reason:         target.action,
   227→              });
   228→            }
   229→            // A idles for the normal decision interval (fallback handled by Engine wander)
   230→            character.wanderTimer = this.decisionIntervalMs;
   231→            break;
   232→          }
   233→
   234→          // Release any previous object reservation before walking to talk
   235→          if (character.targetObjectId) {
   236→            const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);
   237→            if (prevObj) prevObj.release(character.name);
   238→            character.targetObjectId = null;
   239→          }
   240→
   241→          // Request Phase: walk A to a tile adjacent to B.
   242→          // Arrival handler in Engine will start the shared talking state.
   243→          const adjTile = simState?.findAdjacentWalkable?.(Math.round(target.tx), Math.round(target.ty));
   244→          if (adjTile && character.moveTo(adjTile.tx, adjTile.ty)) {
   245→            // Store pending talk so Engine knows what to do on arrival
   246→            character.pendingTalkTarget   = target;
   247→            character.pendingTalkDialogue = decision.dialogue       || null;
   248→            character.pendingTalkResponse = decision.targetDialogue || null;
   249→          }
   250→        }
   251→        break;
   252→      }
   253→    }
   254→
   255→    // For talk actions, dialogue is held until both characters enter talking state.
   256→    if (decision.dialogue && decision.action !== 'talk') {
   257→      character.currentDialogue = decision.dialogue;
   258→      character.dialogueTimer   = 5000;
   259→    }
   260→
   261→    if (decision.description) {
   262→      console.log(`[${character.label}] ${decision.description}`);
   263→    }
   264→
   265→    if (this.onDecision) {
   266→      this.onDecision(character, decision, objects, simState);
   267→    }
   268→  }
   269→}

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

object is currently interactable\n   187\u2192          const stateDef = obj.states[obj.state];\n   188\u2192          if (!obj.isFull() && stateDef?.transitions?.use) {\n   189\u2192            // Walk to an adjacent walkable tile (same pattern as wander engine)\n   190\u2192            const adjTile = simState?.findAdjacentWalkable?.(obj.tx, obj.ty);\n   191\u2192            if (adjTile && character.moveTo(adjTile.tx, adjTile.ty)) {\n   192\u2192              if (obj.reserve(character.name)) { // claim slot; guard against any race\n   193\u2192                character.targetObjectId = obj.id;\n   194\u2192                character.pendingAction  = 'use';\n   195\u2192              }\n   196\u2192            }\n   197\u2192          }\n   198\u2192        }\n   199\u2192        break;\n   200\u2192      }\n   201\u2192\n   202\u2192      case 'idle':\n   203\u2192        // Release any pending reservation, then stay put for one full decision interval\n   204\u2192        if (character.targetObjectId) {\n   205\u2192          const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);\n   206\u2192          if (prevObj) prevObj.release(character.name);\n   207\u2192          character.targetObjectId = null;\n   208\u2192        }\n   209\u2192        character.wanderTimer = this.decisionIntervalMs;\n   210\u2192        break;\n   211\u2192\n   212\u2192      case 'talk': {\n   213\u2192        const target = characters.find(c => c.label === decision.targetCharacter);\n   214\u2192        if (target) {\n   215\u2192          // Evaluate Phase: check if B can accept a conversation request.\n   216\u2192          // Accept if idle or walking; reject if interacting, talking, or otherwise busy.\n   217\u2192          const canAccept = target.action === 'idle' || target.action === 'walking';\n   218\u2192\n   219\u2192          if (!canAccept) {\n   220\u2192            // B is busy \u2014 log the rejection and let A fall back to idle/wander\n   221\u2192            if (simState?.pushEvent) {\n   222\u2192              simState.pushEvent({\n   223\u2192                type:           'talk_rejected',\n   224\u2192                initiatorLabel: character.label,\n   225\u2192                targetLabel:    target.label,\n   226\u2192                reason:         target.action,\n   227\u2192              });\n   228\u2192            }\n   229\u2192            // A idles for the normal decision interval (fallback handled by Engine wander)\n   230\u2192            character.wanderTimer = this.decisionIntervalMs;\n   231\u2192            break;\n   232\u2192          }\n   233\u2192\n   234\u2192          // Release any previous object reservation before walking to talk\n   235\u2192          if (character.targetObjectId) {\n   236\u2192            const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);\n   237\u2192            if (prevObj) prevObj.release(character.name);\n   238\u2192            character.targetObjectId = null;\n   239\u2192          }\n   240\u2192\n   241\u2192          // Request Phase: walk A to a tile adjacent to B.\n   242\u2192          // Arrival handler in Engine will start the shared talking state.\n   243\u2192          const adjTile = simState?.findAdjacentWalkable?.(Math.round(target.tx), Math.round(target.ty));\n   244\u2192          if (adjTile && character.moveTo(adjTile.tx, adjTile.ty)) {\n   245\u2192            // Store pending talk so Engine knows what to do on arrival\n   246\u2192            character.pendingTalkTarget   = target;\n   247\u2192            character.pendingTalkDialogue = decision.dialogue       || null;\n   248\u2192            character.pendingTalkResponse = decision.targetDialogue || null;\n   249\u2192          }\n   250\u2192        }\n   251\u2192        break;\n   252\u2192      }\n   253\u2192    }\n   254\u2192\n   255\u2192    // For talk actions, dialogue is held until both characters enter talking state.\n   256\u2192    if (decision.dialogue && decision.action !== 'talk') {\n   257\u2192      character.currentDialogue = decision.dialogue;\n   258\u2192      character.dialogueTimer   = 5000;\n   259\u2192    }\n   260\u2192\n   261\u2192    if (decision.description) {\n   262\u2192      console.log(`[${character.label}] ${decision.description}`);\n   263\u2192    }\n   264\u2192\n   265\u2192    if (this.onDecision) {\n   266\u2192      this.onDecision(character, decision, objects, simState);\n   267\u2192    }\n   268\u2192  }\n   269\u2192}\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"
  }
}
ASSISTANTMESSAGE #66
Now I have a complete picture. Here's my diagnosis: **Root Cause**: The fallback events from `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` are almost never triggered because: 1. The slot reservation system (FRG-222) prevents characters from ever arriving at unavailable objects 2. `_doWander` and `AIEngine._applyDecision` both check `isFull()` and state before assigning 3. Objects in 'idle' state have no `auto_next`, so they can't transition mid-walk **The visible "redirects"** QA observes are from `AIEngine._applyDecision` when: - The AI sends a character to a different object than they were heading to - The AI sends a character to idle/move/talk (abandoning their object target) These redirect paths don't emit fallback events. **Fix**: I need to modify `AIEngine._applyDecision` to emit `'fallback'` events via `simState.pushEvent` whenever the AI redirects a character—whether that's switching them to a different object, sending them to idle when their target becomes full, or abandoning an object target entirely to move freely. I also need to update `debug.html` to render the new 'plan_changed' reason. Let me create a feature branch and start implementing these changes.
▶ VIEW RAW DATA 
{
  "content": {
    "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": "Now I have a complete picture. Here's my diagnosis:\n\n**Root Cause**: The fallback events from `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` are almost never triggered because:\n1. The slot reservation system (FRG-222) prevents characters from ever arriving at unavailable objects\n2. `_doWander` and `AIEngine._applyDecision` both check `isFull()` and state before assigning\n3. Objects in 'idle' state have no `auto_next`, so they can't transition mid-walk\n\n**The visible \"redirects\"** QA observes are from `AIEngine._applyDecision` when:\n\n\n- The AI sends a character to a different object than they were heading to\n- The AI sends a character to idle/move/talk (abandoning their object target)\n\nThese redirect paths don't emit fallback events.\n\n**Fix**: I need to modify `AIEngine._applyDecision` to emit `'fallback'` events via `simState.pushEvent` whenever the AI redirects a character\u2014whether that's switching them to a different object, sending them to idle when their target becomes full, or abandoning an object target entirely to move freely.\n\nI also need to update `debug.html` to render the new 'plan_changed' reason. Let me create a feature branch and start implementing these changes.",

ASSISTANTMESSAGE #67
Now I understand the bug clearly. The root cause: 1. `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` is almost **never called** in normal operation because the slot reservation system (FRG-222) prevents characters from ever arriving at unavailable objects — `_doWander` and `AIEngine._applyDecision` both check `isFull()` before targeting 2. The **visible redirects** QA observes happen in `AIEngine._applyDecision` when the AI reassigns characters mid-walk — but no fallback events are emitted there Let me create the branch and fix:
▶ VIEW RAW DATA 
{
  "content": {
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Now I understand the bug clearly. The root cause:\n\n1. `_fallbackToAlternativeObject` is almost **never called** in normal operation because the slot reservation system (FRG-222) prevents characters from ever arriving at unavailable objects \u2014 `_doWander` and `AIEngine._applyDecision` both check `isFull()` before targeting\n2. The **visible redirects** QA observes happen in `AIEngine._applyDecision` when the AI reassigns characters mid-walk \u2014 but no fallback events are emitted there\n\nLet me create the branch and fix:"
  }
}

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/home/gem/project/src/AIEngine.js",
  "old_string": "      case 'move':\n        if (decision.targetTile) {\n          // Release any pending object reservation before moving freely\n          if (character.targetObjectId) {\n            const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);\n            if (prevObj) prevObj.release(character.name);\n            character.targetObjectId = null;\n          }\n          character.moveTo(decision.targetTile.tx, decision.targetTile.ty);\n        }\n        break;\n\n      case 'interact': {\n        const obj = objects.find(o => o.id === decision.targetId);\n        if (obj) {\n          // If already heading to a different object, release that reservation first\n          if (character.targetObjectId && character.targetObjectId !== obj.id) {\n            const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);\n            if (prevObj) prevObj.release(character.name);\n            character.targetObjectId = null;\n          }\n\n          // Only commit if a slot is available and object is currently interactable\n          const stateDef = obj.states[obj.state];\n          if (!obj.isFull() && stateDef?.transitions?.use) {\n            // Walk to an adjacent walkable tile (same pattern as wander engine)\n            const adjTile = simState?.findAdjacentWalkable?.(obj.tx, obj.ty);\n            if (adjTile && character.moveTo(adjTile.tx, adjTile.ty)) {\n              if (obj.reserve(character.name)) { // claim slot; guard against any race\n                character.targetObjectId = obj.id;\n                character.pendingAction  = 'use';\n              }\n            }\n          }\n        }\n        break;\n      }\n\n      case 'idle':\n        // Release any pending reservation, then stay put for one full decision interval\n        if (character.targetObjectId) {\n          const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);\n          if (prevObj) prevObj.release(character.name);\n          character.targetObjectId = null;\n        }\n        character.wanderTimer = this.decisionIntervalMs;\n        break;\n\n      case 'talk': {\n        const target = characters.find(c => c.label === decision.targetCharacter);\n        if (target) {\n          // Evaluate Phase: check if B can accept a conversation request.\n          // Accept if idle or walking; reject if interacting, talking, or otherwise busy.\n          const canAccept = target.action === 'idle' || target.action === 'walking';\n\n          if (!canAccept) {\n            // B is busy \u2014 log the rejection and let A fall back to idle/wander\n            if (simState?.pushEvent) {\n              simState.pushEvent({\n                type:           'talk_rejected',\n                initiatorLabel: character.label,\n                targetLabel:    target.label,\n                reason:         target.action,\n              });\n            }\n            // A idles for the normal decision interval (fallback handled by Engine wander)\n            character.wanderTimer = this.decisionIntervalMs;\n            break;\n          }\n\n          // Release any previous object reservation before walking to talk\n          if (character.targetObjectId) {\n            const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);\n            if (prevObj) prevObj.release(character.name);\n            character.targetObjectId = null;\n          }",
  "new_string": "      case 'move':\n        if (decision.targetTile) {\n          // Release any pending object reservation before moving freely\n          if (character.targetObjectId) {\n            const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);\n            if (prevObj) {\n              prevObj.release(character.name);\n              if (simState?.pushEvent) {\n                simState.pushEvent({\n                  type:      'fallback',\n                  charLabel: character.label,\n                  reason:    'plan_changed',\n                  fromLabel: prevObj.label ?? prevObj.name,\n                  toLabel:   null,\n                });\n              }\n            }\n            character.targetObjectId = null;\n          }\n          character.moveTo(decision.targetTile.tx, decision.targetTile.ty);\n        }\n        break;\n\n      case 'interact': {\n        const obj = objects.find(o => o.id === decision.targetId);\n        if (obj) {\n          // If already heading to a different object, release that reservation first\n          let abandonedLabel = null;\n          if (character.targetObjectId && character.targetObjectId !== obj.id) {\n            const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);\n            if (prevObj) {\n              prevObj.release(character.name);\n              abandonedLabel = prevObj.label ?? prevObj.name;\n            }\n            character.targetObjectId = null;\n          }\n\n          // Only commit if a slot is available and object is currently interactable\n          const stateDef = obj.states[obj.state];\n          if (!obj.isFull() && stateDef?.transitions?.use) {\n            // Walk to an adjacent walkable tile (same pattern as wander engine)\n            const adjTile = simState?.findAdjacentWalkable?.(obj.tx, obj.ty);\n            if (adjTile && character.moveTo(adjTile.tx, adjTile.ty)) {\n              if (obj.reserve(character.name)) { // claim slot; guard against any race\n                character.targetObjectId = obj.id;\n                character.pendingAction  = 'use';\n                if (abandonedLabel && simState?.pushEvent) {\n                  simState.pushEvent({\n                    type:      'fallback',\n                    charLabel: character.label,\n                    reason:    'plan_changed',\n                    fromLabel: abandonedLabel,\n                    toLabel:   obj.label ?? obj.name,\n                  });\n                }\n              }\n            }\n          } else if (abandonedLabel && simState?.pushEvent) {\n            // New target unavailable \u2014 character goes idle after abandoning previous\n            simState.pushEvent({\n              type:      'fallback',\n              charLabel: character.label,\n              reason:    'object_unavailable',\n              fromLabel: abandonedLabel,\n              toLabel:   null,\n            });\n          }\n        }\n        break;\n      }\n\n      case 'idle':\n        // Release any pending reservation, then stay put for one full decision interval\n        if (character.targetObjectId) {\n          const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);\n          if (prevObj) {\n            prevObj.release(character.name);\n            if (simState?.pushEvent) {\n              simState.pushEvent({\n                type:      'fallback',\n                charLabel: character.label,\n                reason:    'plan_changed',\n                fromLabel: prevObj.label ?? prevObj.name,\n                toLabel:   null,\n              });\n            }\n          }\n          character.targetObjectId = null;\n        }\n        character.wanderTimer = this.decisionIntervalMs;\n        break;\n\n      case 'talk': {\n        const target = characters.find(c => c.label === decision.targetCharacter);\n        if (target) {\n          // Evaluate Phase: check if B can accept a conversation request.\n          // Accept if idle or walking; reject if interacting, talking, or otherwise busy.\n          const canAccept = target.action === 'idle' || target.action === 'walking';\n\n          if (!canAccept) {\n            // B is busy \u2014 log the rejection and let A fall back to idle/wander\n            if (simState?.pushEvent) {\n              simState.pushEvent({\n                type:           'talk_rejected',\n                initiatorLabel: character.label,\n                targetLabel:    target.label,\n                reason:         target.action,\n              });\n            }\n            // A idles for the normal decision interval (fallback handled by Engine wander)\n            character.wanderTimer = this.decisionIntervalMs;\n            break;\n          }\n\n          // Release any previous object reservation before walking to talk\n          if (character.targetObjectId) {\n            const prevObj = objects.find(o => o.id === character.targetObjectId);\n            if (prevObj) {\n              prevObj.release(character.name);\n              if (simState?.pushEvent) {\n                simState.pushEvent({\n                  type:      'fallback',\n                  charLabel: character.label,\n                  reason:    'plan_changed',\n                  fromLabel: prevObj.label ?? prevObj.name,\n                  toLabel:   target.label,\n                });\n              }\n            }\n            character.targetObjectId = null;\n          }"
}
